message.content:
Adversaries may exploit the AppCert DLLs mechanism to achieve persistence and elevate privileges by loading malicious DLLs into processes. This technique leverages the AppCertDLLs Registry key, causing specified DLLs to be loaded into every process that invokes common API functions like CreateProcess and WinExec. By injecting malicious DLLs, attackers can execute code with elevated privileges within the context of various processes, similar to process injection methods. This can lead to continuous persistence as the malicious DLLs are repeatedly triggered by API activity. Known malware such as PUNCHBUGGY has utilized this method to maintain a foothold on compromised systems.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and auditing the AppCertDLLs Registry key for unauthorized changes, employing application whitelisting to prevent the execution of unapproved DLLs, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect and block suspicious DLL loading activities. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce the risk of exploitation through this technique.
